## Summary
The hard problem of consciousness, formulated by Chalmers (1995), asks why and how physical processes in the brain give rise to subjective experience. Unlike "easy problems" of explaining cognitive functions, the hard problem concerns why there is "something it is like" to be conscious at all.

## Evidence
The explanatory gap between physical descriptions and phenomenal experience resists functional analysis. Even a complete neuroscientific account of visual processing would not explain why red looks the way it does. Thought experiments (zombies, Mary's room, inverted qualia) illustrate the gap between functional and phenomenal properties.

## Significance
The hard problem defines the central challenge in philosophy of mind and has shaped two decades of consciousness research. It motivates both deflationary responses (illusionism, eliminativism) and expansionary metaphysics (panpsychism, property dualism), structuring the entire field.
